key_insight: Removing one unmarked edge from a complete graph causes a provable, small but systematic perturbation in the Szegedy quantum walk spectrum, with the gap eigenvalue shifting downward and the effect scaling as ~1/n^2 in the search-relevant regime.
engineering_relevance: This gives a theoretical foundation for quantum-assisted integrity monitoring of dense communication networks, where detecting a single missing link is the minimal and hardest structural anomaly.
method: Perturb the Szegedy quantum walk transition matrix for a complete graph with marked nodes and one missing unmarked-unmarked edge.
Bound the perturbation norm as Θ(1/n) and track its effect on the gap eigenvalue, eigenphase, and effective subspace geometry.
Analyze the marked-node regime m=Θ(n) to derive scaling laws for eigenphase shift, rotation angle, and success probability.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports analytic bounds and asymptotic scaling results, including a strictly negative first-order eigenvalue shift for all n and m, plus Θ(1/n^2) and O(1/√n) performance changes in the m=Θ(n) regime.
watch_item: The strongest guarantees are asymptotic and depend on the dense-graph, single-edge-defect setting, so practical performance may differ on sparse or noisier networks.
paper_title: Single Link Removal Perturbation in Szegedy Quantum Walk: from Graph Completeness Testing to Integrity Monitoring
